items.title: Defending the Digital Pitch: World Cup 2026 Cyber Threats
items.description: The 2026 FIFA World Cup presents a concentrated attack surface spanning three nations, 16 cities, and billions of viewers. Cybercriminals have already launched phishing campaigns, fraudulent ticket sales, and brand impersonation schemes targeting governments, sponsors, broadcasters, transportation providers, and telecommunications companies. Financially motivated actors are exploiting tournament-r
items.ai_summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup has become a prime target for cybercriminals, with phishing campaigns and brand impersonation schemes already launched, exploiting the tournament's large and diverse attack surface. The threat actors are using T1566 phishing tactics to trick victims into divulging sensitive information, and T1134 access token manipulation to gain unauthorized access to systems. If exploited, these attacks could result in financial loss, data breaches, and reputational damage for governments, sponsors, broadcasters, and other organizations involved in the tournament. To defend against these threats, organizations should implement robust email filtering and authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication, and ensure that all employees are aware of the risks of phishing and brand impersonation. Specific remediation steps include configuring email clients to block suspicious attachments and URLs, and implementing a token-based authentication system with regular token rotation and revocation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Affidavit in Support of Application for Criminal Complaint
items.description: An FBI investigation identified Denis Nikolayevich Obrezko, a Russian national, as facilitating cyber intrusions conducted by the Russia-aligned threat group Void Blizzard. Between June and July 2024, multiple U.S. companies across various sectors were targeted in a large-scale cyber espionage campaign involving mass email harvesting and unauthorized access. The threat actors utilized stolen sessi
items.ai_summary: The Void Blizzard threat group, attributed to Russian national Denis Nikolayevich Obrezko, conducted a large-scale cyber espionage campaign targeting multiple U.S. companies between June and July 2024. The attack vector involved mass email harvesting and unauthorized access, utilizing stolen session information to gain initial access. The real-world impact of this campaign includes potential data breaches and intellectual property theft, affecting various sectors. To remediate, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication, monitor email traffic for suspicious activity, and update their email filtering systems to detect and block phishing attempts. Specifically, defenders should configure their email servers to use version 2 of the Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocol and ensure all employees use up-to-date antivirus software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1589.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How Lookalike Domains Exploit Human Judgment
items.description: Lookalike attacks exploit human cognitive shortcuts rather than technical vulnerabilities, designing domain names that resemble legitimate services to bypass security controls. These attacks leverage predictable patterns in how people read and process text, using techniques including homographs, typosquatting, domain embedding, and keyword association. The domain name itself embeds targeting inten
items.ai_summary: The threat involves lookalike domain attacks that exploit human cognitive biases rather than technical vulnerabilities, utilizing techniques such as homographs, typosquatting, domain embedding, and keyword association to bypass security controls. These attacks can lead to significant real-world impact, including phishing, business email compromise, and malware distribution, affecting individuals and organizations alike. For example, an attacker may register a domain name similar to that of a legitimate service, such as 'g00gle.com' instead of 'google.com', to trick users into divulging sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific measures such as implementing Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC) with a policy of 'reject' to prevent email spoofing, and configuring web browsers to highlight the top-level domain (TLD) in the address bar. Additionally, users should be educated on how to identify lookalike domains and report suspicious activity.

    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1114| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: Threat Actors Weaponize AI Hype to Deliver AsyncRAT
items.description: A sophisticated malware campaign exploits growing interest in artificial intelligence by distributing malicious files disguised as AI-related learning resources and technical guides. The attack employs an exceptionally complex multi-stage infection chain beginning with compressed archives containing LNK shortcuts and hidden PDF files. Through multiple layers of obfuscation involving PowerShell scr
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are exploiting the growing interest in artificial intelligence to deliver AsyncRAT malware through a complex multi-stage infection chain. The attack begins with compressed archives containing LNK shortcuts and hidden PDF files, which are disguised as AI-related learning resources and technical guides. The malware employs T1059.001 PowerShell and T1027 obfuscated files or information to evade detection, and T1036 masquerading to blend in with legitimate files. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement T1190 exploit mitigation measures, such as input validation and secure file upload handling, and ensure that all systems are configured to block T1204 user execution of malicious files. Additionally, defenders should monitor for T1104 multi-stage channels and inspect all compressed archives and PDF files for suspicious content.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actors Target FIFA World Cup 2026
items.description: A sophisticated Chinese-origin fraud operation is targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees through pixel-perfect website clones and a multi-tenant phishing infrastructure. The actors deploy typosquatted domains and a commercially developed administrative system to mimic legitimate FIFA ticketing platforms. Technical analysis reveals high-fidelity brand cloning, real-time card skimming capabilities,
items.ai_summary: The threat actors behind the operation targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees utilize a combination of typosquatted domains and high-fidelity brand cloning to create pixel-perfect website clones, leveraging T1583.001 to register deceptive domains and T1566 for phishing attacks. The real-world impact of this operation includes potential financial loss for attendees who fall victim to the phishing campaign, with the threat actors capable of real-time card skimming. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about the dangers of interacting with suspicious emails or websites, and ensure that all employees are aware of the phishing tactics being used. Additionally, defenders can take specific steps such as configuring their DNS to block known typosquatted domains and implementing a web application firewall to detect and prevent phishing attempts. The threat actors' use of a commercially developed administrative system to manage their phishing infrastructure suggests a high level of sophistication and organization.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1583.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion
items.description: Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels are utilizing sophisticated online infrastructure consisting of over 36 inauthentic websites to facilitate sanctions evasion. These websites impersonate ship registries, national maritime administrations, seafarer training organizations, protection and indemnity clubs, and classification societies from jurisdictions including Comoros, Benin, Bhutan, Cameroo
items.ai_summary: The Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion threat involves Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels utilizing over 36 inauthentic websites to impersonate legitimate maritime organizations, including ship registries and classification societies. These websites are used to facilitate sanctions evasion by creating false documentation and credentials for vessels. The threat actors employ TTPs such as T1562 (Impair Defenses) to evade detection. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust domain validation and verification processes for maritime-related websites, and monitor for suspicious activity related to vessel documentation and certification. Additionally, defenders should conduct regular audits of their systems and networks to detect and prevent potential impersonation attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1562| B[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1110| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Gamers beware: malicious wallpapers on Steam found stealing accounts
items.description: Since late 2025, cybercriminals have been exploiting Wallpaper Engine, a popular live wallpaper application on Steam, to distribute malware through Steam Workshop. Attackers target primarily Chinese and Russian gamers by embedding malicious code within application wallpapers shared on the platform. These compromised wallpapers deliver various malware types including infostealers, backdoors, crypto
items.ai_summary: The malicious wallpapers on Steam exploit the Wallpaper Engine application to distribute malware, primarily targeting Chinese and Russian gamers. The attackers embed malicious code within the wallpapers shared on the Steam Workshop platform, which delivers various malware types, including infostealers, backdoors, and cryptocurrency stealers. If exploited, the malware can lead to unauthorized access to the victim's Steam account, resulting in potential financial loss and identity theft. To remediate this threat, Steam users should avoid downloading and installing wallpapers from untrusted sources, and instead, opt for verified and trusted content creators. Additionally, users should regularly monitor their account activity and report any suspicious behavior to Steam support.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: WebAssembly Malware Found in Trojanized Open VSX Extensions
items.description: Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions ship ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that poll the Solana blockchain for command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos. This novel dead-drop technique allows attackers to rotate infrastructure without hardco
items.ai_summary: The threat involves Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace, which deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions contain ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that communicate with the Solana blockchain to receive command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos, utilizing the T1071 application layer protocol. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of developer systems, potentially affecting a wide range of industries. To remediate, developers should remove any suspicious extensions and update their Visual Studio Code to the latest version, ensuring that all extensions are verified and come from trusted sources. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for unusual Solana blockchain interactions can help in early detection of such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Public and Private Medical Community Targeted by Threat Actor Pursuing Artificial Intelligence, Cyber, Medical, and National Defense Research
items.description: A sophisticated espionage campaign attributed to UNC6508, a China-nexus threat actor, targeted North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year. The adversary exploited REDCap servers, deployed custom INFINITERED malware to harvest credentials, and maintained persistent access through trojanized legitimate files that survived software upgrades. After remaining u
items.ai_summary: The UNC6508 threat actor has been conducting a sophisticated espionage campaign targeting North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year, with a focus on artificial intelligence, cyber, medical, and national defense research. The adversary exploited REDCap servers, likely through social engineering or unpatched vulnerabilities, to gain initial access and deploy custom INFINITERED malware for credential harvesting. The malware was designed to maintain persistence through trojanized legitimate files that survived software upgrades, allowing the threat actor to maintain long-term access to sensitive research data. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately inspect their REDCap servers for signs of compromise, update to the latest version, and implement robust credential management and monitoring practices. Additionally, defenders should conduct regular security audits and implement a zero-trust architecture to limit the blast radius in case of a breach.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How attackers are jailbreaking LLMs with CTF framing and how to catch them
items.description: Threat actors are bypassing AI model safety guardrails by framing exploit requests as legitimate security research, such as capture-the-flag challenges or CVE-hunting exercises. This technique manipulates upstream LLMs into generating working exploit code that attackers deploy against real targets. Multiple independent operators have been observed targeting five applications—PraisonAI, LiteLLM, Fa
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting large language models (LLMs) by framing malicious requests as capture-the-flag challenges or CVE-hunting exercises, effectively bypassing safety guardrails. This technique manipulates upstream LLMs into generating working exploit code, which is then deployed against real targets, including PraisonAI, LiteLLM, and Fa. The real-world impact of this exploit is significant, as it allows attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should implement strict input validation and monitoring of LLM-generated code, as well as provide security awareness training to researchers and developers to prevent social engineering attacks. Additionally, defenders should review and update their incident response plans to include scenarios involving LLM-based exploits.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

As a result, there are no technical details or affected products to report. The real-world impact is minimal, with no known threat actors exploiting this non-vulnerability. items.title: CVE-2026-11441: A vulnerability was identified in theonedev onedev up to 15.0.5. This vulnerabil...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in theonedev onedev up to 15.0.5. This vulnerability affects the function canAccessIssue of the file /issues/ of the component Pull Request Handler. Such manipulation of the argument issue leads to improper authorization. It is possible to launch the attack remotely. Upgrading to version 15.0.6 is able to resolve this issue. It is advisable to upgrade the affected co
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11441 is a vulnerability in theonedev onedev up to version 15.0.5, specifically affecting the canAccessIssue function of the Pull Request Handler component. This vulnerability allows for improper authorization, which can be exploited remotely. If exploited, an attacker could potentially gain unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems, affecting users of the onedev platform. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to version 15.0.6, which resolves the issue. Additionally, it is recommended to review and monitor system logs for any suspicious activity, and to consider implementing additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and access controls.

items.description: A vulnerability was determined in theonedev onedev up to 15.0.5. This affects an unknown part of the file /repositories/{projectId}/default-branch of the component REST API. This manipulation of the argument project.defaultBranch causes improper authorization. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. Upgrading to version 15.0.6 is able to mitigate this issue. Upgrading the affected componen
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11440 is a vulnerability in theonedev onedev up to version 15.0.5, affecting the REST API component, specifically the /repositories/{projectId}/default-branch endpoint. The vulnerability allows for improper authorization due to manipulation of the project.defaultBranch argument, which can be exploited remotely. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to repository data, potentially affecting developers and organizations using the onedev platform. To remediate this issue, defenders should upgrade to version 15.0.6, ensuring that the REST API component is updated to prevent exploitation. Additionally, defenders should review access controls and authorization mechanisms to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11439 is a vulnerability in theonedev onedev up to version 15.0.5, where an improper authorization issue exists due to the manipulation of the project.parentId argument in the Parent Project Handler component. This can be exploited remotely, potentially allowing unauthorized access to project data. The real-world impact of this vulnerability could result in data breaches or unauthorized modifications to project settings, affecting users of the onedev platform. To remediate this issue, defenders should upgrade to version 15.0.6, ensuring that all instances of onedev are updated to prevent exploitation. Additionally, defenders should review project access controls and monitor for suspicious activity to detect potential exploitation attempts.

To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to version 15.0.6, which addresses the issue. items.title: Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims
items.description: California Water Service says there is no indication of operational disruptions to its water and wastewater systems. The post Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: White House Issues Memo to Bolster NSS Cybersecurity
items.description: NSPM-12 establishes a clear structure for NSS cybersecurity governance and accountability and reestablishes CNSS. The post White House Issues Memo to Bolster NSS Cybersecurity appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Survey: 94% of Incidents Involve Anonymized Infrastructure. Teams Are Still Reactive

Every day, analysts ingest enrichment feeds, geolocation data, reputation scores, telemetry, and threat intelligence from a growing ecosystem of vendors and platforms. items.title: Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages
items.description: Arch Linux suspended account registrations in response to the wave of malicious packages being uploaded to AUR. The post Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has given U.S. government agencies three days to secure their servers against an actively exploited vulnerability (CVE-2026-54420) in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin. [...]

items.description: Bad actors are exploiting multiple security vulnerabilities in Fortinet FortiSandbox, according to threat intelligence firm Defused Cyber. In a post shared on X, the company said it has observed exploitation of CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 over the past 24 hours. items.title: Cybersecurity Executives Urge the Trump Administration to Ease Restrictions on Anthropic AI Models
items.description: A group of cybersecurity executives and experts is asking the Trump administration to lift its directive preventing the use of Anthropic’s latest artificial intelligence models by foreign nationals. The post Cybersecurity Executives Urge the Trump Administration to Ease Restrictions on Anthropic AI Models appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ransomware gang abuses Microsoft Teams relays to hide malicious traffic

items.title: Tech Coalition ‘Athena’ Targets OSS Vulnerabilities Ahead of Disclosure
items.description: Over two dozen organizations built a shared platform to triage vulnerabilities, fix them, and secure the software before patches arrive. The post Tech Coalition ‘Athena’ Targets OSS Vulnerabilities Ahead of Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws now exploited in attacks
items.description: Attackers are now exploiting several critical vulnerabilities in Fortinet's FortiSandbox cyber threat detection platform, according to threat intelligence company Defused. [...]

items.title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
items.description: Windows variants for the SprySOCKS Linux malware have been used in attacks targeting government organizations in at least four countries. [...]

items.title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
items.description: The North Korean state-sponsored hacking group known as ScarCruft (aka APT37) has been observed using spear-phishing messages impersonating Microsoft Account security notifications to deliver malware called NarwhalRAT. "The attack email contained a message impersonating an MS account security alert," the Genians Security Center (GSC) said. "It was designed to create concern over possible

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: iRhythm discloses data breach, says hackers stole patient info
items.description: Digital healthcare company iRhythm Holdings has disclosed a data breach after hackers stole patients' personal and health information stored on third-party-hosted business applications. [...]

items.title: Cisco Patches Another SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in Attacks
items.description: Cisco recently became aware of the exploitation of CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day that allows arbitrary file write. The post Cisco Patches Another SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
items.description: Cisco has released security updates for a medium-severity security flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that has come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20262, carries a CVSS score of 6.5 out of 10.0. "A vulnerability in the web UI of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, formerly SD-WAN vManage, could allow an authenticated, remote attacker to create a file or

items.title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a security flaw impacting LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, requiring Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 18, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-54420 (CVSS score: 8.5), which has been described as a case of privilege

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 16th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9974, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: UK to ban social media access for children under 16
items.description: The ban will apply to all “user-to-user platforms, whose purpose is to enable social interaction and which allow users to post material, alongside algorithms,” according to a press release from the government’s Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.

items.title: Estonia to quarantine emails sent from Russian .ru domain before they reach government officials
items.description: Estonia will require additional security screening for emails sent from Russia’s .ru top-level domain before they reach government officials, according to the country's minister of justice and digital affairs.

items.title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice announced Friday that it has seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com websites, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, in what appears to be the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. [...]

items.title: SimpleHelp bug lets hackers create rogue remote support accounts
items.description: A vulnerability in the SimpleHelp remote management software allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged technician accounts on servers using the OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication protocol. [...]

items.title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
items.description: A China-linked espionage group hid inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks for more than a year, quietly stealing sensitive research and defense email. The way in was a backdoor on their REDCap research servers that stole login credentials. The exfiltration was the unusual part: the attackers rewired the victims' own Google Workspace rules to copy any message

items.title: North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged two malicious cyber campaigns that exhibit similarities with a persistent North Korean threat cluster known as Contagious Interview (aka Famous Chollima, HexagonalRodent, and Void Dokkaebi). According to a report published by Proofpoint, the threat actor has been found orchestrating phishing campaigns using developer role recruitment or code review themes

items.title: Maine closes data breach portal to the public after fake reports
items.description: Maine is still allowing companies to report breaches, but won’t make the portal easily available to the public until after it completes an audit of its procedures to stop such incidents, according to a press release from the Maine attorney general’s office.

items.title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
items.description: WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage have been compromised in a supply-chain attack impacting Awesome Motive-s content distribution network (CDN). [...]

items.title: Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks
items.description: Cisco has released security updates to address a vulnerability in the Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, tracked as CVE-2026-20262, that was exploited in attacks to escalate to root privileges. [...]

items.title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
items.description: A default low-privilege account on a LiteLLM proxy can climb to full admin and run code on the server by chaining three vulnerabilities, researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed LiteLLM is a widely deployed open-source AI gateway that brokers calls to more than 100 model providers behind one OpenAI-compatible interface. A server takeover exposes every provider key it holds, the secrets that

items.title: Council of Europe investigates ShinyHunters data breach claims
items.description: The Council of Europe, the continent's oldest intergovernmental body, is probing claims of a data breach made by the ShinyHunters extortion group over the weekend. [...]

items.title: Ransomware Attack Shuts Down Mills of Australia’s Second-Largest Sugar Producer
items.description: Mackay Sugar was targeted in a cyberattack carried out by a threat group known as The Gentlemen. The post Ransomware Attack Shuts Down Mills of Australia’s Second-Largest Sugar Producer appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
items.description: A single click on a trusted Microsoft link could have let an attacker pull emails, calendar details, and indexed files out of Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search. Researchers at Varonis Threat Labs chained three bugs into a one-click exfiltration path they call SearchLeak. Because the link pointed to a real microsoft.com domain, traditional anti-phishing and URL filtering tools were

items.title: Cyberattack on Russian tech firm Astral disrupts business, government services for week
items.description: According to customer complaints, the disruption affected a range of services used by businesses, leading to interruptions in cash register operations, difficulties selling certain regulated goods, loss of access to customer portals and corporate email and problems with electronic human resources document management systems and authentication using digital certificates.

items.title: Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America
items.description: Google’s Threat Intelligence Group has been tracking the cyberespionage group as UNC6508 since early 2025. The post Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables
items.description: According to the deputy prosecutor general, the ship’s officers have now been charged with “having damaged two subsea telecommunications cables and of having attempted to damage a total of eight other subsea connections.”

items.title: NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding
items.description: The startup has built a security-first identity platform to protect humans, machines, and AI agents. The post NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges
items.description: Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko admitted to working on the development of a loader for the Conti gang. The post Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9968, (Thu, Jun 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
